homeostasis.q: Which body cavity contains the brain and spinal cord?
homeostasis.tip: The DORSAL cavity = cranial (brain) + vertebral (spinal cord). The VENTRAL cavity = thoracic + abdominopelvic.

homeostasis.q: A coronal (frontal) plane divides the body into:
homeostasis.tip: CORONAL = front/back (anterior/posterior). SAGITTAL = left/right. TRANSVERSE = top/bottom.

homeostasis.q: The term 'distal' means:
a: Closer to the point of origin/attachment
Farther from the point of origin/attachment
homeostasis.tip: DISTAL = farther from attachment. PROXIMAL = closer. Think: distal = distant from origin.

homeostasis.q: Body temperature is primarily regulated by the:
homeostasis.tip: The HYPOTHALAMUS is the body's thermostat — it detects temp changes and triggers sweating or shivering.

homeostasis.q: The diaphragm separates which two cavities?
homeostasis.tip: The DIAPHRAGM is the muscular floor of the thoracic cavity, separating it from the abdominopelvic cavity.

homeostasis.q: In anatomical position, the palms face:
homeostasis.tip: Standard anatomical position: standing upright, feet together, arms at sides, palms FORWARD (anteriorly).

homeostasis.q: A sagittal plane divides the body into:
homeostasis.tip: SAGITTAL = left/right. A MIDSAGITTAL plane divides equally; PARASAGITTAL is off-center.

homeostasis.q: The term 'superficial' means:
homeostasis.tip: SUPERFICIAL = near the surface. DEEP = farther from surface. Skin is superficial to muscle.

homeostasis.q: The chemical level of organization involves:
homeostasis.tip: The chemical level is the LOWEST — atoms combine to form molecules (proteins, DNA) which make up cells.

homeostasis.q: Which directional term is opposite to 'medial'?
homeostasis.tip: MEDIAL = toward midline. LATERAL = away from midline. The ears are lateral to the nose.

homeostasis.q: Blood clotting (coagulation) is an example of:
homeostasis.tip: POSITIVE feedback AMPLIFIES the change. Clotting factors recruit more clotting factors until a clot forms — then it stops.

homeostasis.q: The four basic tissue types in the body are:
homeostasis.tip: Four tissue types: EPITHELIAL (covers/lines), CONNECTIVE (supports), MUSCLE (movement), NERVOUS (communication).

homeostasis.q: 'Superior' is synonymous with which directional term?
homeostasis.tip: SUPERIOR = CEPHALIC = toward the head. INFERIOR = CAUDAL = toward the tail/feet.

homeostasis.q: The set point in a homeostatic system is:
a: The ideal target value the body maintains
homeostasis.tip: SET POINT = the normal value (like 37°C for temp). The control system works to keep the variable AT the set point.

homeostasis.q: A transverse (horizontal) plane cuts the body into:
homeostasis.tip: TRANSVERSE (cross-section) = superior/inferior. Like cutting a loaf of bread into top and bottom slices.

homeostasis.q: Metabolism includes:
a: Neither — metabolism refers only to breathing
homeostasis.tip: METABOLISM = all chemical reactions. ANABOLISM builds (uses energy). CATABOLISM breaks down (releases energy).

homeostasis.q: The ventral body cavity is subdivided into the thoracic cavity and the:
homeostasis.tip: VENTRAL cavity = thoracic + abdominopelvic. The abdominopelvic is further divided into abdominal and pelvic.

homeostasis.q: 'Prone' refers to the body lying:
homeostasis.tip: PRONE = face DOWN. SUPINE = face UP. Remember: supine = on your spine (back).

homeostasis.q: The control center in a homeostatic feedback loop:
a: Receives and integrates information to determine the response
Is always the same as the receptor
homeostasis.tip: CONTROL CENTER (e.g., brain/hypothalamus) = integrator. It compares the stimulus to the set point and directs the effector.

homeostasis.q: The term 'contralateral' means:
homeostasis.tip: CONTRALATERAL = opposite side. IPSILATERAL = same side. The right brain controls contralateral (left) body.

homeostasis.q: Which is the correct anatomical order from smallest to largest?
a: Chemical → Cellular → Tissue → Organ → Organ system → Organism
homeostasis.tip: Chemical → Cellular → Tissue → Organ → Organ System → Organism. Each level builds on the previous.

homeostasis.q: Physiology is best defined as the study of:
homeostasis.tip: ANATOMY = structure. PHYSIOLOGY = function. They are inseparable — structure determines function.

homeostasis.q: The pleural cavities contain the:
homeostasis.tip: Each PLEURAL cavity surrounds one lung. The PERICARDIAL cavity surrounds the heart. Both are within the thoracic cavity.

homeostasis.q: Which of the following is a characteristic of life?
homeostasis.tip: Characteristics of life include: responsiveness, growth, reproduction, metabolism, homeostasis, and organization.

homeostasis.q: The hypogastric region of the abdominopelvic cavity is located:
homeostasis.tip: 9-region model: hypogastric = inferior middle (pelvic area). Epigastric = superior middle. Umbilical = center.

cytology.q: The powerhouse of the cell responsible for ATP production is the:
cytology.tip: MITOCHONDRIA produce ATP via aerobic respiration. They have a double membrane — the inner membrane folds (cristae) house ATP synthase.

cytology.q: Which organelle is the primary site of protein synthesis?
cytology.tip: RIBOSOMES translate mRNA into protein. Free ribosomes make cytoplasmic proteins; ribosomes on rough ER make secretory proteins.

cytology.q: DNA replication occurs during which phase of the cell cycle?
cytology.tip: S PHASE = DNA synthesis/replication. G1 = growth/prep. G2 = final prep before division. M = actual cell division.

cytology.q: Active transport differs from passive transport because it:
a: Moves substances along their concentration gradient
Requires ATP to move substances against their gradient
cytology.tip: ACTIVE transport uses ATP to move substances AGAINST their concentration gradient (low to high). Passive transport is downhill (no energy).

cytology.q: The Golgi apparatus functions to:
a: Package, modify, and ship proteins to their destinations
cytology.tip: GOLGI = cell's post office. It receives proteins from rough ER, modifies them (adds sugars), then packages and ships them.

cytology.q: Lysosomes contain _____ that digest cellular waste:
cytology.tip: LYSOSOMES are membrane-bound sacs containing digestive enzymes at acidic pH (~5). They break down worn organelles (autophagy) and debris.

cytology.q: During mitosis, chromosomes align at the cell's equator during:
cytology.tip: PMAT: Prophase (condense), METAPHASE (align at middle), Anaphase (pull apart), Telophase (two nuclei reform).

cytology.q: Osmosis is the movement of:
a: Solutes from low to high concentration
Water across a semipermeable membrane from high to low water concentration
cytology.tip: OSMOSIS = water moves from HYPOTONIC (dilute, more water) to HYPERTONIC (concentrated, less water) across a semipermeable membrane.

cytology.q: Which type of endocytosis engulfs large solid particles such as bacteria?
cytology.tip: PHAGOCYTOSIS = cell eating (solid particles). PINOCYTOSIS = cell drinking (fluid droplets). Used by immune cells to engulf pathogens.

cytology.q: A hypertonic solution causes a cell to:
cytology.tip: HYPERTONIC = more solutes outside cell → water LEAVES the cell → cell SHRINKS (crenation). Isotonic = no net movement.

cytology.q: Which phase of mitosis involves the nuclear envelope re-forming?
cytology.tip: TELOPHASE: chromosomes reach poles, nuclear envelopes reform, chromosomes decondense, and cytokinesis begins.

cytology.q: The cell membrane is primarily composed of a:
cytology.tip: The cell membrane = PHOSPHOLIPID BILAYER with embedded proteins (fluid mosaic model). Hydrophilic heads face outward; hydrophobic tails face inward.

cytology.q: Gap junctions between cells allow:
a: Direct cytoplasmic communication via small molecules
cytology.tip: GAP JUNCTIONS = protein channels (connexins) that directly connect adjacent cells cytoplasm. Important in heart muscle coordination.

cytology.q: Apoptosis is best described as:
cytology.tip: APOPTOSIS = programmed cell death. It is orderly and normal (e.g., removing webbing between fetal fingers). NECROSIS = accidental, messy death.

cytology.q: Rough ER is 'rough' because of attached:
cytology.tip: ROUGH ER has ribosomes studding its surface → gives bumpy appearance. It synthesizes proteins destined for secretion or membranes.

cytology.q: A cell placed in an isotonic solution will:
a: Maintain normal size with no net osmosis
cytology.tip: ISOTONIC = same solute concentration as the cell. No net water movement → cell size stays constant. IV saline (0.9% NaCl) is isotonic.

cytology.q: Microvilli on cell surfaces primarily function to:
a: Greatly increase surface area for absorption
cytology.tip: MICROVILLI = tiny finger-like projections of the plasma membrane that dramatically increase surface area for absorption (e.g., intestinal cells).

cytology.q: During anaphase of mitosis, what moves to opposite poles?
a: Sister chromatids pulled apart at the centromere
cytology.tip: ANAPHASE: sister chromatids are pulled apart by spindle fibers to opposite poles. Each pole will become a new cell's nucleus.

cytology.q: The cytoskeleton provides the cell with:
a: Shape, structural support, and enables movement
Energy in the form of ATP
cytology.tip: CYTOSKELETON (microfilaments, microtubules, intermediate filaments) gives cells shape, anchors organelles, and enables movement and division.

cytology.q: Smooth ER is specialized for:
a: Lipid synthesis, detoxification, and calcium storage
cytology.tip: SMOOTH ER lacks ribosomes. Functions: lipid synthesis, drug/toxin detoxification (liver), and Ca2+ storage (muscle cells).

cytology.q: Receptor-mediated endocytosis is used to bring what into the cell?
a: Specific molecules bound to membrane receptors (e.g., cholesterol)
cytology.tip: Receptor-mediated endocytosis is SELECTIVE — receptors on the cell surface bind specific molecules (e.g., LDL cholesterol), then the membrane folds inward.

cytology.q: The G2/M checkpoint in the cell cycle ensures:
a: DNA replication is complete and accurate before mitosis
The cell has grown large enough
cytology.tip: G2/M checkpoint = quality control before mitosis. If DNA is damaged or incompletely replicated, the cell cycle is halted for repair.

cytology.q: Cilia and flagella are made of:
cytology.tip: Cilia and flagella contain MICROTUBULES in a 9+2 arrangement (9 doublets + 2 central). Dynein motors cause bending movement.

cytology.q: The nucleolus inside the nucleus is responsible for:
a: Ribosomal RNA synthesis and ribosome subunit assembly
cytology.tip: The NUCLEOLUS is a dense region within the nucleus. It transcribes rRNA genes and assembles ribosome subunits that are exported to the cytoplasm.

cytology.q: Peroxisomes function to:
a: Break down fatty acids and detoxify harmful substances using H2O2
cytology.tip: PEROXISOMES use oxygen to break down fatty acids and neutralize toxins. They produce H2O2 as a byproduct, which they immediately break down with catalase.

integumentary.q: The outermost layer of the epidermis is the:
integumentary.tip: STRATUM CORNEUM = outermost, dead, keratin-filled cells. They slough off and are replaced from below. Layers from outside in: Corneum, Lucidum, Granulosum, Spinosum, Basale.

integumentary.q: Melanin is produced by which specialized epidermal cells?
integumentary.tip: MELANOCYTES produce melanin (the pigment) and transfer it to surrounding KERATINOCYTES. Melanin protects against UV radiation.

integumentary.q: Which skin layer contains blood vessels and nerve endings?
integumentary.tip: The DERMIS is vascular and innervated. The EPIDERMIS has NO blood vessels (avascular) — it depends on diffusion from dermal capillaries.

integumentary.q: Eccrine sweat glands are distributed across most of the body and primarily function in:
integumentary.tip: ECCRINE glands produce watery sweat for cooling. They are everywhere, especially palms/soles. APOCRINE glands (armpits/groin) activate at puberty.

integumentary.q: The dermis is primarily composed of what type of tissue?
integumentary.tip: DERMIS = dense irregular connective tissue (collagen + elastic fibers). This gives skin strength and elasticity. Dermis also contains hair follicles and glands.

integumentary.q: Vitamin D synthesis in the skin requires exposure to:
integumentary.tip: UV radiation converts a cholesterol derivative in the skin to vitamin D3 precursor, which is then activated by the liver and kidneys.

integumentary.q: The hypodermis (subcutaneous layer) is primarily composed of:
a: Adipose and loose areolar connective tissue
integumentary.tip: HYPODERMIS = subcutaneous fat + loose connective tissue. It insulates, stores energy, and anchors skin to underlying muscle and bone.

integumentary.q: Sebaceous glands secrete:
integumentary.tip: SEBACEOUS glands produce SEBUM (oil) into hair follicles. Sebum lubricates skin/hair and has mild antimicrobial properties.

integumentary.q: A first-degree burn (superficial burn) damages:
a: Epidermis and part of the dermis
integumentary.tip: 1st degree: epidermis only (red, painful, no blisters — like a sunburn). 2nd degree: epidermis + dermis (blisters). 3rd degree: full thickness.

integumentary.q: The stratum basale is significant because it contains:
a: The outermost cells of the epidermis
integumentary.tip: STRATUM BASALE = deepest epidermal layer. Contains stem cells that continually divide, pushing cells upward to replace lost surface cells.

integumentary.q: Nails are composed of:
integumentary.tip: Nails = hard keratin (same protein as skin/hair, but more heavily cross-linked). The nail matrix at the base produces new nail cells.

integumentary.q: Pacinian corpuscles (lamellated corpuscles) are sensitive to:
integumentary.tip: PACINIAN corpuscles: deep pressure + vibration. MEISSNER'S: light touch. FREE NERVE ENDINGS: pain + temperature. RUFFINI: skin stretch.

integumentary.q: Skin color is determined by the combination of:
a: Only the number of melanocytes present
integumentary.tip: Skin color = MELANIN (brown/yellow/red) + HEMOGLOBIN in blood (pink/red) + CAROTENE (orange pigment from diet). All three contribute.

integumentary.q: Third-degree burns are characterized by:
a: Full-thickness destruction — skin, nerves, often painless at site
integumentary.tip: 3rd degree (full thickness): destroys all skin layers + nerve endings → often PAINLESS at site. Requires skin grafting. Scarring is severe.

integumentary.q: Arrector pili muscles in the dermis cause:
a: Goosebumps (piloerection) in response to cold or fear
integumentary.tip: ARRECTOR PILI = tiny smooth muscle attached to hair follicle. Contracts in cold/fear → hair stands up (goosebumps). Vestigial heat/threat response.

integumentary.q: Langerhans cells (dendritic cells) in the epidermis function as:
a: Immune cells that detect and present antigens
integumentary.tip: LANGERHANS CELLS = epidermal macrophages/dendritic cells. They patrol the skin for pathogens, engulf them, and present antigens to T lymphocytes.

integumentary.q: The rule of nines is a clinical tool used to:
a: Estimate the percentage of body surface area affected by burns
integumentary.tip: RULE OF NINES: Head=9%, each arm=9%, chest=9%, abdomen=9%, each leg=18%, back=18%, perineum=1%. Total = 100%. Used for burn severity assessment.

integumentary.q: Apocrine sweat glands are concentrated in the:
integumentary.tip: APOCRINE glands: axilla + groin + areola. Activated at puberty. Secrete thick, protein-rich sweat. Bacteria break it down → body odor.

integumentary.q: The nail root is located:
a: At the free edge of the nail
Under the nail body in the middle
Deep to the proximal nail fold (hidden under skin)
integumentary.tip: NAIL ROOT is hidden under the skin at the proximal end. The NAIL MATRIX (just distal to root) produces new nail cells. LUNULA is the white crescent.

integumentary.q: Which type of skin cancer arises from melanocytes and is the most dangerous?
integumentary.tip: MELANOMA arises from melanocytes and is highly metastatic (most dangerous). BASAL CELL is most common but least dangerous. Use the ABCDE rule for detection.

integumentary.q: Merkel cells (tactile discs) in the stratum basale detect:
a: Light sustained touch and texture discrimination
integumentary.tip: MERKEL CELLS = slow-adapting receptors for sustained light touch and fine texture/shape discrimination. Most dense in fingertips and lips.

integumentary.q: The epidermis is classified as which type of epithelium?
integumentary.tip: Epidermis = STRATIFIED SQUAMOUS KERATINIZED epithelium. Multiple layers protect against abrasion; keratin waterproofs and strengthens.

integumentary.q: Which layer of the dermis is responsible for fingerprints?
integumentary.tip: PAPILLARY LAYER (superficial dermis) has fingerprint ridges (dermal papillae) that interlock with epidermal ridges — creating unique fingerprints.

integumentary.q: Cyanosis (bluish discoloration of skin) indicates:
integumentary.tip: CYANOSIS = blue/purple skin from low blood oxygen (deoxygenated hemoglobin is dark purple). Seen in heart failure, respiratory failure, cold exposure.

integumentary.q: The first phase of wound healing is:
a: Hemostasis — bleeding is stopped via platelet plug and clotting
integumentary.tip: Wound healing phases: 1) HEMOSTASIS (clot), 2) INFLAMMATION (immune cells), 3) PROLIFERATION (new cells/vessels), 4) REMODELING (collagen maturation).

skeletal.q: The medullary cavity of long bones contains _____ in adults:
skeletal.tip: In adults: YELLOW marrow (fat) fills the medullary cavity. In children/active red marrow sites (sternum, ribs, vertebrae), RED marrow makes blood cells.

skeletal.q: Osteoclasts are cells that:
skeletal.tip: OSTEOCLASTS = bone EATERS (resorption). They dissolve bone mineral with acid and enzymes. Balance between osteoclast/osteoblast activity determines bone density.

skeletal.q: Compact bone is organized into repeating cylindrical units called:
skeletal.tip: OSTEONS (Haversian systems) = functional units of compact bone. Central Haversian canal (blood/nerves) surrounded by concentric lamellae of mineralized matrix.

skeletal.q: Mature bone cells that maintain bone matrix and live in lacunae are called:
skeletal.tip: OSTEOCYTES = mature bone cells living in LACUNAE. They maintain matrix and communicate via CANALICULI (tiny channels). They sense mechanical stress.

skeletal.q: A synovial joint is characterized by the presence of:
a: A fluid-filled joint cavity lined with synovial membrane
skeletal.tip: SYNOVIAL joints have a JOINT CAVITY filled with SYNOVIAL FLUID (lubricates). Most limb joints are synovial. Types: hinge, ball-and-socket, pivot, etc.

skeletal.q: The axial skeleton includes all of the following EXCEPT:
skeletal.tip: AXIAL skeleton = skull + vertebral column + thoracic cage (80 bones). APPENDICULAR = limbs + girdles (126 bones). Remember: axial = axis/central.

skeletal.q: Endochondral ossification is the process where:
a: Bone forms directly within fibrous membrane
A cartilage model is gradually replaced by bone
Blood vessels invade cartilage and destroy it without replacement
Marrow forms without a cartilage precursor
skeletal.tip: ENDOCHONDRAL ossification: cartilage model → bone (most bones). INTRAMEMBRANOUS ossification: bone forms directly in fibrous tissue (flat skull bones).

skeletal.q: Red bone marrow carries out:
skeletal.tip: RED MARROW = hematopoietic tissue that produces ALL blood cells (RBCs, WBCs, platelets). Found in spongy bone of flat bones, vertebrae, and proximal long bones.

skeletal.q: The epiphyseal plate (growth plate) is composed of:
skeletal.tip: Growth plates are HYALINE CARTILAGE. Chondrocytes here proliferate and hypertrophy, lengthening the bone. They ossify when growth stops (epiphyseal line).

skeletal.q: A ball-and-socket joint (like the hip or shoulder) allows:
a: Multiaxial movement in all directions (most freedom of all joint types)
skeletal.tip: BALL-AND-SOCKET joints = most mobile (multiaxial). Examples: hip and shoulder. Allow flexion, extension, abduction, adduction, rotation, circumduction.

skeletal.q: Which mineral is MOST abundant in bone matrix?
skeletal.tip: Bone = organic matrix (collagen) + inorganic salts. HYDROXYAPATITE (Ca10(PO4)6(OH)2) provides hardness and rigidity. About 65% of bone is mineral.

skeletal.q: A hinge joint (like the elbow) allows:
skeletal.tip: HINGE joints are UNIAXIAL (one plane). Elbow: flexion/extension. Knee: mainly flexion/extension + slight rotation. Ankle: dorsiflexion/plantarflexion.

skeletal.q: The carpal bones of the wrist are classified as:
skeletal.tip: SHORT BONES (cube-shaped): carpals and tarsals. LONG BONES: limb bones. FLAT BONES: skull, sternum, ribs. IRREGULAR: vertebrae, facial bones.

skeletal.q: Parathyroid hormone (PTH) acts on bone and kidneys to:
a: Increase blood calcium by stimulating bone resorption and renal Ca2+ reabsorption
skeletal.tip: PTH raises blood calcium: stimulates OSTEOCLASTS (bone resorption), increases renal Ca2+ reabsorption, and activates vitamin D. Calcitonin does the opposite.

skeletal.q: Osteoporosis is caused by:
a: Bone resorption exceeding bone formation (net bone loss)
skeletal.tip: OSTEOPOROSIS: osteoclast activity > osteoblast activity → net BONE LOSS → decreased density → fracture risk. Common after menopause (estrogen protects bone).

skeletal.q: The adult vertebral column contains how many bones total?
a: 26 bones total (24 movable vertebrae + sacrum + coccyx)
skeletal.tip: 26 vertebral bones: 7 cervical + 12 thoracic + 5 lumbar + 1 sacrum (5 fused) + 1 coccyx (3-4 fused). 24 PRESACRAL movable vertebrae.

skeletal.q: Which of the following is a bone DEPRESSION, not a projection?
skeletal.tip: PROJECTIONS: condyle, trochanter, tubercle, spine, crest. DEPRESSIONS/OPENINGS: fossa, foramen, sulcus, fovea. Fossa is a HOLLOW, not a projection.

skeletal.q: Bursae are fluid-filled sacs that function to:
a: Reduce friction where tendons or ligaments pass over bone
skeletal.tip: BURSAE = small synovial sacs near joints. Reduce friction between tendons, ligaments, and bone. Inflamed bursae = BURSITIS (painful joint condition).

skeletal.q: The fontanelles of an infant's skull are:
a: Fibrous membrane-covered gaps between cranial bones
skeletal.tip: FONTANELLES = soft spots on infant skull. Allow head compression during birth and rapid brain growth. Anterior fontanelle closes ~18 months; posterior ~2-3 months.

skeletal.q: Scoliosis is:
a: An abnormal lateral (sideways) curvature of the spine
skeletal.tip: SCOLIOSIS = lateral (sideways) S- or C-curve. LORDOSIS = exaggerated lumbar curve (swayback). KYPHOSIS = exaggerated thoracic curve (hunchback).

skeletal.q: Cartilage differs from bone in that cartilage:
a: Is avascular (no blood vessels) and heals slowly
skeletal.tip: CARTILAGE is AVASCULAR — nutrients diffuse through matrix. This is why cartilage heals slowly after injury. Contains chondrocytes in lacunae within matrix.

skeletal.q: The olecranon process is a bony prominence of which bone?
skeletal.tip: The OLECRANON is the bony point of the elbow — it is the proximal process of the ULNA. You can feel it when you bend your elbow.

skeletal.q: Gomphoses are fibrous joints found only in:
a: Tooth sockets (periodontal ligament anchoring teeth to jaw)
skeletal.tip: GOMPHOSIS = peg-in-socket fibrous joint. Only example in the body: teeth anchored in jawbone by periodontal ligament. Virtually immovable.

skeletal.q: The greater trochanter is a large bony prominence on the:
skeletal.tip: GREATER TROCHANTER of the FEMUR = attachment point for gluteal muscles. You can feel it on the lateral hip. Lesser trochanter is on the medial/posterior side.

skeletal.q: In fibrocartilage, the matrix contains:
a: Abundant thick collagen bundles providing high tensile strength
skeletal.tip: FIBROCARTILAGE = abundant collagen → very tough and resistant to compression. Found in intervertebral discs, menisci of knee, pubic symphysis.

muscular.q: The functional unit of skeletal muscle contraction is the:
muscular.tip: SARCOMERE = functional unit of contraction. It runs from Z-disc to Z-disc and contains interdigitating thick (myosin) and thin (actin) filaments.

muscular.q: Which ion, released from the sarcoplasmic reticulum, triggers muscle contraction?
muscular.tip: Action potential → Ca2+ released from SARCOPLASMIC RETICULUM → Ca2+ binds TROPONIN → tropomyosin shifts → actin active sites exposed → myosin binds → contraction.

muscular.q: Slow-twitch (Type I) muscle fibers are best adapted for:
a: High power output and rapid fatigue
muscular.tip: TYPE I (slow-twitch): many mitochondria, myoglobin, high aerobic capacity, fatigue-resistant. For marathon running, posture. TYPE II (fast-twitch): power/speed but fatigue quickly.

muscular.q: The neuromuscular junction is the synapse between:
a: A motor neuron axon terminal and a muscle fiber
Two muscle fibers at the Z-disc
A sensory neuron and a muscle fiber
muscular.tip: NMJ = where motor neuron meets muscle. ACh released → binds nicotinic receptors on motor end plate → depolarizes sarcolemma → action potential → contraction.

muscular.q: Tropomyosin's role at rest in muscle is to:
a: Cover actin's active sites, blocking myosin binding
muscular.tip: At rest, TROPOMYOSIN physically blocks myosin-binding sites on actin. Ca2+ binds troponin → troponin moves tropomyosin off → sites exposed → cross-bridge cycle begins.

muscular.q: Creatine phosphate (phosphocreatine) provides energy for:
a: The first 10-15 seconds of intense exercise (rapid ATP regeneration)
muscular.tip: Creatine phosphate + ADP → ATP + creatine (creatine kinase). Very fast but limited supply (~10-15 seconds). Used in sprinting and weightlifting bursts.

muscular.q: Cardiac muscle is unique because it is:
a: Involuntary, striated, and connected by intercalated discs
muscular.tip: CARDIAC muscle: involuntary, striated (sarcomeres), and connected by INTERCALATED DISCS (gap junctions + desmosomes). This allows synchronized contraction.

muscular.q: The H-zone in a sarcomere:
a: Contains only thick filaments with no thin filament overlap
muscular.tip: H-ZONE = center of sarcomere where only THICK (myosin) filaments exist — no thin filament overlap. It NARROWS during contraction. A-band stays constant.

muscular.q: Acetylcholine (ACh) released at the NMJ binds to receptors on:
a: The sarcolemma at the motor end plate
muscular.tip: ACh crosses the synaptic cleft → binds NICOTINIC receptors on the SARCOLEMMA (motor end plate) → Na+ flows in → muscle cell depolarizes → contraction begins.

muscular.q: Eccentric muscle contraction occurs when a muscle:
muscular.tip: ECCENTRIC: muscle lengthens under load (e.g., lowering a dumbbell). Causes most DOMS (delayed onset muscle soreness). CONCENTRIC: shortens. ISOMETRIC: same length.

muscular.q: Rigor mortis occurs after death because:
a: ATP depletion means myosin cannot detach from actin
Nerve signals continue briefly after death
muscular.tip: Rigor mortis: no ATP → myosin LOCKS onto actin (cannot detach). Peaks ~12 hours; resolves in ~48-72 hours as proteins degrade.

muscular.q: A motor unit consists of:
a: One muscle and all its innervating nerves
One motor neuron and ALL the muscle fibers it innervates
One muscle fiber and its NMJ
muscular.tip: MOTOR UNIT = 1 motor neuron + all muscle fibers it controls. Fine control muscles (eye) have few fibers/neuron. Power muscles have hundreds/neuron.

muscular.q: Isometric muscle contraction means:
a: Constant muscle length with changing tension — no joint movement
Force produced equals the load, causing shortening
muscular.tip: ISOMETRIC: muscle generates force but does NOT shorten (joint does not move). E.g., pushing against a wall. Muscle tension increases but length stays the same.

muscular.q: The sliding filament theory states that during contraction:
a: Thin (actin) filaments slide toward the center over thick (myosin) filaments — sarcomere shortens
muscular.tip: SLIDING FILAMENT: neither actin nor myosin filaments actually shorten — actin slides over myosin toward the M-line. The SARCOMERE (Z-Z distance) shortens.

muscular.q: Lactic acid accumulates in muscle during:
a: Intense anaerobic exercise when pyruvate exceeds aerobic capacity
muscular.tip: During intense exercise, aerobic metabolism cannot keep up → anaerobic glycolysis → pyruvate → LACTIC ACID. Contributes to burning sensation and fatigue.

muscular.q: Which connective tissue layer surrounds each individual muscle fiber?
muscular.tip: ENDOMYSIUM surrounds each fiber. PERIMYSIUM surrounds fascicles (bundles). EPIMYSIUM surrounds entire muscle. All are continuous with the TENDON.

muscular.q: Acetylcholinesterase (AChE) at the NMJ functions to:
a: Rapidly break down ACh in the synaptic cleft to terminate the signal
muscular.tip: AChE breaks down ACh in the synaptic cleft → repolarization → muscle relaxes. Without AChE, ACh accumulates → sustained contraction (nerve gas mechanism).

muscular.q: Physiological tetanus in muscle physiology refers to:
a: A sustained smooth maximal contraction from fused high-frequency stimulation
muscular.tip: PHYSIOLOGICAL TETANUS: stimuli so rapid that muscle cannot relax between them → sustained smooth maximal contraction. Not the same as tetanus infection (Clostridium).

muscular.q: What is the role of titin in the sarcomere?
a: Acts as a molecular spring, stabilizing the thick filament and contributing to passive elasticity
Hydrolyzes ATP for the power stroke
Blocks active sites on actin at rest
muscular.tip: TITIN is the largest protein in the body. It anchors myosin to the Z-disc, acting as a SPRING that resists overstretching and helps sarcomeres return to resting length.

muscular.q: Oxygen debt (EPOC) after exercise refers to:
a: Oxygen stored in myoglobin during rest
The extra O2 consumed after exercise to restore ATP, creatine phosphate, and remove lactate
A decrease in O2-carrying capacity after exertion
muscular.tip: EPOC (Excess Post-exercise Oxygen Consumption) = elevated O2 use AFTER exercise to replenish creatine phosphate, convert lactate to glucose, and restore normal metabolism.

muscular.q: The zone of a sarcomere that does NOT change in length during contraction is the:
a: A-band (length of thick filaments stays constant)
muscular.tip: A-BAND = width of thick (myosin) filaments = stays CONSTANT. I-band and H-zone SHORTEN during contraction as actin slides inward.

muscular.q: Smooth muscle differs from skeletal muscle in that smooth muscle:
a: Is involuntary, non-striated, and contains no sarcomeres
muscular.tip: SMOOTH muscle: no sarcomeres/striations, no T-tubules, uses calmodulin (not troponin) to regulate contraction, involuntary. Found in hollow organs and blood vessels.

muscular.q: A muscle twitch is:
a: A single, brief muscle contraction in response to one action potential
Slow recruitment of multiple motor units over time
muscular.tip: A TWITCH = the mechanical response to ONE action potential: latent period → contraction → relaxation. Multiple twitches summate to produce stronger/sustained contractions.

muscular.q: What connects muscle to bone?
muscular.tip: TENDON connects muscle to bone. LIGAMENT connects bone to bone. APONEUROSIS is a flat sheet-like tendon (e.g., connecting abdominal muscles). All are dense regular CT.

muscular.q: Muscle fatigue during sustained activity is caused by multiple factors EXCEPT:
a: Depletion of ATP and creatine phosphate
Accumulation of inorganic phosphate and H+ ions
Depletion of ACh at the NMJ
Increased calcium release from sarcoplasmic reticulum
muscular.tip: Muscle fatigue causes: ATP/CP depletion, lactate/H+ accumulation, ACh vesicle depletion, impaired Ca2+ release. INCREASED Ca2+ release would promote contraction, not fatigue.

nervous.q: Myelin in the PNS is produced by:
nervous.tip: PNS myelin: SCHWANN CELLS (one cell myelinates one segment of one axon). CNS myelin: OLIGODENDROCYTES (one cell myelinates many axon segments).

nervous.q: The resting membrane potential of a typical neuron is approximately:
nervous.tip: -70 mV = resting potential. Inside is NEGATIVE relative to outside. Maintained by Na+/K+ ATPase (3 Na+ out, 2 K+ in) and K+ leak channels.

nervous.q: The part of the neuron that receives most incoming signals is the:
nervous.tip: DENDRITES receive incoming signals and carry them toward the cell body. The AXON carries impulses AWAY from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

nervous.q: During depolarization of a neuron, the membrane becomes highly permeable to:
nervous.tip: DEPOLARIZATION: voltage-gated Na+ channels open → Na+ RUSHES IN → inside becomes positive (~+30mV). The membrane overshoots threshold.

nervous.q: Saltatory conduction in myelinated neurons means that action potentials:
a: Travel continuously along the entire axon
Jump from node to node of Ranvier (faster conduction)
Conduct more slowly due to insulation
nervous.tip: SALTATORY conduction: impulse jumps node to node of Ranvier. MUCH FASTER than continuous conduction. Myelin insulates between nodes.

nervous.q: The parasympathetic division of the ANS is associated with:
a: Rest-and-digest; conserving energy and promoting recovery
Increasing heart rate and blood pressure
nervous.tip: PARASYMPATHETIC: rest-and-digest. Slows heart, increases digestion, constricts pupils, promotes urination/defecation. Craniosacral origin.

nervous.q: The cerebellum is primarily responsible for:
a: Coordinating movement, balance, and fine motor control
nervous.tip: CEREBELLUM: coordination, balance, posture, and fine motor learning. Does NOT initiate movement but REFINES it. Damage → ataxia (uncoordinated movement).

nervous.q: An IPSP (inhibitory postsynaptic potential) makes a neuron:
a: More likely to fire an action potential
Less likely to fire by hyperpolarizing the membrane
nervous.tip: IPSP: inhibitory neurotransmitter (e.g., GABA) → K+ out or Cl- in → membrane becomes MORE negative (hyperpolarized) → harder to reach threshold → inhibition.

nervous.q: The blood-brain barrier (BBB) is formed primarily by:
a: Tight junctions between brain capillary endothelial cells and astrocyte end-feet
nervous.tip: BBB: TIGHT JUNCTIONS between endothelial cells + astrocyte end-feet. Restricts passage of most substances. Allows: O2, CO2, glucose, lipid-soluble drugs. Blocks most pathogens.

nervous.q: The sympathetic nervous system originates from which region of the spinal cord?
a: Cranial nerves and sacral spinal cord
nervous.tip: SYMPATHETIC: THORACOLUMBAR origin (T1-L2). Short preganglionic, long postganglionic. Neurotransmitter: NOREPINEPHRINE (mostly). PARASYMPATHETIC: craniosacral origin.

nervous.q: The occipital lobe of the cerebrum is primarily responsible for:
nervous.tip: Lobes: FRONTAL (motor, planning, personality). PARIETAL (somatosensory, spatial). TEMPORAL (hearing, memory, language). OCCIPITAL (vision).

nervous.q: A simple reflex arc typically contains:
a: Receptor → afferent neuron → integration center → efferent neuron → effector
Two sensory neurons and one motor neuron only
nervous.tip: REFLEX ARC: receptor (detects stimulus) → afferent (sensory) neuron → integration center (spinal cord) → efferent (motor) neuron → effector (muscle/gland).

nervous.q: GABA is the main _____ neurotransmitter in the CNS:
nervous.tip: GABA (gamma-aminobutyric acid) = major INHIBITORY neurotransmitter in CNS. Opens Cl- channels → hyperpolarization. Benzodiazepines (Valium) enhance GABA. Glutamate is the main excitatory.

nervous.q: The hypothalamus regulates all of the following EXCEPT:
nervous.tip: HYPOTHALAMUS: temperature, hunger, thirst, circadian rhythms, emotions, controls pituitary. Higher cognition = PREFRONTAL CORTEX. Hypothalamus is subcortical.

nervous.q: Repolarization of a neuron after an action potential involves:
a: K+ flowing OUT of the cell, restoring negative inside
nervous.tip: REPOLARIZATION: voltage-gated K+ channels open (delayed) → K+ flows OUT → inside becomes negative again. Slight HYPERPOLARIZATION (afterpotential) may follow.

nervous.q: The limbic system is associated with:
nervous.tip: LIMBIC SYSTEM: amygdala (fear/emotions), hippocampus (memory formation), cingulate gyrus. Involved in emotion, motivation, and forming declarative memories.

nervous.q: The vagus nerve (CN X) is responsible for:
a: Most parasympathetic visceral control (heart, lungs, GI tract)
nervous.tip: VAGUS NERVE = cranial nerve X. The main parasympathetic nerve — controls heart rate, breathing, and GI function. 80% of its fibers are AFFERENT (sensory from viscera).

nervous.q: Multiple sclerosis (MS) involves destruction of:
a: Motor neuron cell bodies in the spinal cord
nervous.tip: MS = autoimmune DEMYELINATION of CNS myelin (oligodendrocytes attacked). Results in slowed/disrupted nerve conduction → muscle weakness, visual problems, fatigue.

nervous.q: The absolute refractory period after an action potential means:
a: The neuron can fire again with a stronger stimulus
The neuron CANNOT fire another action potential regardless of stimulus strength
The neuron is in a prolonged hyperpolarized state
The neuron is currently depolarizing again
nervous.tip: ABSOLUTE refractory period: Na+ channels are INACTIVATED — the neuron CANNOT fire again no matter how strong the stimulus. Ensures one-directional propagation.

nervous.q: Astrocytes in the CNS perform all of the following EXCEPT:
a: Forming part of the blood-brain barrier
nervous.tip: ASTROCYTES: support, BBB formation, nutrient delivery, regulate K+ and neurotransmitter levels. MYELIN in CNS = OLIGODENDROCYTES. Astrocytes do NOT myelinate.

nervous.q: The thalamus functions as the:
a: Main relay station funneling sensory information to correct cortical areas
nervous.tip: THALAMUS = sensory relay station. Almost ALL sensory information (except smell) synapses in the thalamus before being routed to the correct cortex.

nervous.q: Neurotransmitters are released from:
nervous.tip: Action potential reaches AXON TERMINAL → Ca2+ enters → synaptic vesicles fuse with membrane (exocytosis) → neurotransmitters released into synaptic cleft.

nervous.q: Which division of the nervous system controls voluntary skeletal muscle movement?
nervous.tip: SOMATIC nervous system: voluntary control of SKELETAL muscle. AUTONOMIC: involuntary control of smooth muscle, cardiac muscle, and glands.

nervous.q: The meninges from outermost to innermost are:
a: Dura mater, arachnoid mater, pia mater
Dura mater, pia mater, arachnoid mater
nervous.tip: DAP: DURA mater (tough, outermost) → ARACHNOID mater (middle, spider-web) → PIA mater (delicate, directly on brain). CSF flows in the subarachnoid space.

nervous.q: Long-term potentiation (LTP) in the hippocampus is thought to underlie:
a: Pain modulation in the dorsal horn
nervous.tip: LTP: repeated stimulation strengthens synaptic connections (AMPA/NMDA receptors). The hippocampus uses LTP to form new memories. 'Neurons that fire together, wire together.'

sensation.q: Sensory transduction refers to:
a: Transport of signals along the spinal cord
Conversion of a stimulus into an electrical signal (receptor potential)
Release of neurotransmitters at a synapse
The refractory period of receptor cells
sensation.tip: TRANSDUCTION = converting stimulus energy (light, sound, pressure, chemicals) into an electrical signal the nervous system can use. First step in all sensation.

sensation.q: The fovea centralis in the retina contains the highest density of:
sensation.tip: FOVEA = center of sharp vision. Packed with CONES. No rods here. This is the region of highest visual acuity — where you focus when reading.

sensation.q: The Eustachian (auditory) tube connects the middle ear to the:
sensation.tip: EUSTACHIAN TUBE equalizes pressure between the middle ear and throat. Blocked tubes → ear pressure/pain (e.g., during altitude changes or colds).

sensation.q: Primary olfactory (smell) receptors are located in the:
sensation.tip: OLFACTORY RECEPTORS are bipolar neurons in the roof of the nasal cavity (olfactory epithelium). Their axons pass through the cribriform plate to the olfactory bulb.

sensation.q: Taste receptor cells are found in structures called:
a: Taste buds located within papillae on the tongue
sensation.tip: TASTE BUDS are within PAPILLAE (bumps on tongue). Filiform papillae: no taste buds. Fungiform, circumvallate, and foliate: have taste buds.

sensation.q: The five basic taste modalities include all EXCEPT:
a: Spicy or hot (this is pain, not taste)
sensation.tip: 5 tastes: SWEET, SALTY, SOUR, BITTER, UMAMI. SPICY is NOT a taste — capsaicin activates PAIN/heat nociceptors (TRPV1 channels), not taste receptors.

sensation.q: Rods in the retina are specialized for:
a: High-acuity color vision in bright light
Processing motion only in the central visual field
sensation.tip: RODS: dim light + peripheral vision. One type of photopigment (rhodopsin). CONES: color + high acuity. Three cone types (S, M, L for blue, green, red).

sensation.q: The semicircular canals of the inner ear detect:
sensation.tip: SEMICIRCULAR CANALS: detect ROTATIONAL acceleration. Three canals (horizontal, anterior, posterior) oriented in different planes. Hair cells in ampullae are deflected by endolymph movement.

sensation.q: The cochlea converts sound vibrations into:
a: Nerve impulses sent to the auditory cortex via CN VIII
sensation.tip: COCHLEA: sound waves → basilar membrane vibration → hair cell bending → nerve impulses → auditory nerve (CN VIII) → auditory cortex in temporal lobe.

sensation.q: Accommodation of the lens for near vision involves:
a: Lens flattening due to ciliary muscle relaxation
Ciliary muscles CONTRACTING, causing the lens to THICKEN (increased curvature)
Pupil dilation for more light entry
sensation.tip: Near vision ACCOMMODATION: ciliary muscles CONTRACT → suspensory ligaments RELAX → lens becomes MORE CONVEX (thicker) → greater refractive power. Far vision: opposite.

sensation.q: The sclera of the eye is:
a: The tough white outer fibrous coat of the eye
sensation.tip: EYE LAYERS: FIBROUS (sclera + cornea), VASCULAR (choroid + ciliary body + iris = uvea), NERVOUS (retina). SCLERA = white of the eye.

sensation.q: Proprioception (sense of body and joint position) involves receptors located in:
a: Muscles (muscle spindles), tendons (Golgi tendon organs), and joint capsules
sensation.tip: PROPRIOCEPTION: muscle SPINDLES (detect muscle stretch), GOLGI TENDON ORGANS (detect tendon tension), joint receptors. Allows knowing limb position without looking.

sensation.q: The three ossicles of the middle ear in order from eardrum to inner ear are:
sensation.tip: Ossicles transmit sound: MALLEUS (attached to tympanic membrane) → INCUS → STAPES (presses oval window). They amplify sound ~20x as they transmit to inner ear.

sensation.q: Night blindness is associated with a deficiency of:
a: Vitamin A (retinol — needed to make rhodopsin)
sensation.tip: RHODOPSIN (rod photopigment) requires VITAMIN A (retinal) as its chromophore. Vitamin A deficiency → insufficient rhodopsin → poor night/dim vision = night blindness.

sensation.q: Referred pain is pain that is:
a: Always proportional to the degree of tissue damage
Perceived at a location distant from the actual site of injury
sensation.tip: REFERRED PAIN: visceral pain perceived in a DISTANT somatic site. Classic example: heart attack pain felt in left arm/jaw. Visceral and somatic afferents converge on same spinal neurons.

sensation.q: The optic disc (blind spot) is the location where:
a: The optic nerve exits the eye — no photoreceptors here
The lens is anchored to the ciliary body
Color processing begins in the retina
sensation.tip: OPTIC DISC = where ganglion cell axons exit as the optic nerve. No photoreceptors → BLIND SPOT. You do not notice it because the brain fills in the gap.

sensation.q: The retina contains which two types of photoreceptor cells?
sensation.tip: PHOTORECEPTORS: RODS (dim light, peripheral, ~120 million) and CONES (color, acuity, ~6 million, concentrated at fovea). Bipolar/ganglion cells are downstream neurons, not photoreceptors.

sensation.q: The utricle and saccule (otolith organs) of the inner ear detect:
a: Static head position and linear acceleration (gravity)
sensation.tip: OTOLITH ORGANS: maculae contain hair cells topped with otoliths (calcium carbonate crystals). Gravity pulls otoliths → bends hair cells → signals head tilt and linear acceleration.

sensation.q: Nociceptors are sensory receptors specialized for detecting:
sensation.tip: NOCICEPTORS = pain receptors. Free nerve endings that respond to intense mechanical pressure, extreme temperatures, and chemicals released by damaged tissue (bradykinin, substance P).

sensation.q: The pupillary light reflex (bright light causing pupil constriction) is mediated by:
a: CN II (optic) afferent → CN III (oculomotor) efferent via parasympathetic fibers
sensation.tip: PUPILLARY LIGHT REFLEX: light detected by RETINA → CN II (afferent) → Edinger-Westphal nucleus → CN III (efferent, parasympathetic) → constrictor pupillae muscle → MIOSIS.

sensation.q: The auditory cortex is located in the _____ lobe of the cerebrum:
sensation.tip: TEMPORAL lobe: primary auditory cortex (Heschl gyrus). Also contains Wernicke area (language comprehension). OCCIPITAL = vision. PARIETAL = somatosensory. FRONTAL = motor.

sensation.q: Gate control theory of pain proposes that:
a: All pain is proportional to tissue damage
Non-painful input (large fiber stimulation) can close a gate and suppress pain signals in the dorsal horn
Pain signals always take priority over other sensory input
Nociceptors have physical gates that open and close
sensation.tip: GATE CONTROL THEORY (Melzack and Wall): stimulating large-diameter A-beta fibers (rubbing skin) activates interneurons that inhibit pain (C-fiber) transmission in dorsal horn. Explains why rubbing a bruise helps.

sensation.q: Presbyopia is age-related near vision difficulty caused by:
a: Loss of lens elasticity (lens hardens, less able to accommodate)
sensation.tip: PRESBYOPIA: lens gradually loses elasticity with age → less accommodation ability → need reading glasses for near vision. Different from myopia (nearsightedness) or hyperopia.

sensation.q: The spiral organ (organ of Corti) is located in the:
sensation.tip: ORGAN OF CORTI sits on the BASILAR MEMBRANE in the cochlea. Hair cells here are bent by basilar membrane movement → generate electrical signals → sent via CN VIII to brain.

sensation.q: Sensorineural hearing loss differs from conductive hearing loss in that it involves:
a: A blockage of the external ear canal
Damage to the ossicles of the middle ear
Damage to cochlear hair cells or the auditory nerve
Only temporary hearing reduction from fluid
sensation.tip: SENSORINEURAL hearing loss: damage to hair cells (noise, aging, ototoxic drugs) or CN VIII. CONDUCTIVE: physical blockage (earwax, ossicle damage, middle ear fluid). Sensorineural is usually permanent.
